model_narrative: We plan how a fleet of train units circulates through a daily timetable on a rail network. For every type of train unit and every arc of the timetable graph we decide how many units of that type travel along that arc, where the arcs include the scheduled passenger services, the waiting moves that hold stock at a station between events, and the overnight returns that carry stock from the end of one day to the start of the next. Train units come in different types that differ in their seating per class, their number of cars, and their overnight stabling cost. The objective is to minimize the total cost of the train units left stabled overnight.

sets.name: tu
sets.doc: the types of train unit available, each a self-contained set of cars that can be coupled together

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the passenger service classes, namely first class and second class

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the stations in the network

sets.name: g
sets.doc: the arcs of the timetable graph, each a quadruple (origin station, origin time, destination station, destination time); this set unites the in-service arcs, the in-station waiting arcs that connect consecutive events at one station, and the overnight arcs that return from a day's last event back to its first event

sets.name: is_
sets.doc: the in-service arcs, the subset of graph arcs on which trains actually carry passengers between two stations; each is a quadruple (origin station, origin time, destination station, destination time)

sets.name: on
sets.doc: the overnight arcs, the subset of graph arcs that carry stock from the last event of the day at a station back to that station's first event of the day; each is a quadruple (origin station, origin time, destination station, destination time)

sets.name: ste
sets.doc: the station timetable events, each a pair (station, time) marking a moment at a station where stock can arrive or depart

params.name: seats
params.doc: the number of seats of a given service class provided by one unit of a given train-unit type

params.name: numcars
params.doc: the number of cars in one unit of each train-unit type

params.name: cost
params.doc: the cost of keeping one unit of each train-unit type stabled overnight

params.name: demand_param
params.doc: the number of seats of a given service class demanded by passengers on each in-service arc

params.name: maxcars
params.doc: the maximum number of cars permitted on a single in-service train, a scalar

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of units of each train-unit type routed along each graph arc; integer because train units are indivisible

vars.name: obj_var
vars.doc: the total overnight stabling cost, an accounting variable

constraints.name: f[tu,
constraints.intent: Train stock can neither appear nor vanish at any point in the timetable. For each type of train unit and at each station timetable event, the total number of units of that type arriving on all arcs that end at that event must equal the total number of units of that type leaving on all arcs that begin at that event.

constraints.name: demand_param[s,
constraints.intent: Every scheduled service must seat the passengers who want to travel on it. For each in-service arc and each service class, the seats of that class provided by the train units assigned to the arc, summed over all train-unit types, must be at least the demand for that class on that arc.

constraints.name: f[tu,
constraints.intent: No scheduled service may run a train longer than the track allows. For each in-service arc, the total number of cars contributed by the train units assigned to that arc, summed over all train-unit types, must not exceed the maximum number of cars permitted on a single train.

constraints.name: obj_var
constraints.intent: The total stabling cost accounts for every unit left out overnight. Set the total overnight cost equal to the sum over all train-unit types and all overnight arcs of the number of units of that type on that arc times the overnight stabling cost of that type.

query: Maintenance is floating a depot-rotation idea that would hold the overnight tu2 fleet to no more than ten units in total across all depots. We're curious what the circulation cost comes back as if we plan to that ceiling — what does the optimizer return?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_tu2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.f['tu2', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] for a in model.on) <= 10)

query: Crew planners want to keep a small standing reserve of the nimble tu1 units overnight. Say we require at least eight tu1 units stabled across the network each night — what total stabling bill does that scenario produce?
expected_pyomo: model.floor_tu1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.f['tu1', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] for a in model.on) >= 8)

query: There's a stabling-track refit at one depot that would tighten how many of the small tu1 units we can leave out overnight network-wide. If we look at the case where total overnight tu1 stabling is held to at most two units, where does the circulation cost land?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_tu1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.f['tu1', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] for a in model.on) <= 2)

query: The plan leaves a big block of expensive tu2 units sitting overnight — twelve of them. Couldn't we get by stabling at most eight of those big units in total and lean on the smaller tu1s instead? What's the model seeing that makes a leaner tu2 fleet a worse deal?
expected_pyomo: model.lean_tu2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.f['tu2', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] for a in model.on) <= 8)

query: We end up holding five tu1 units overnight. Wouldn't it be cleaner to keep at least ten of those small units around for morning flexibility? Why does the optimizer resist parking more of the cheap tu1s overnight?
expected_pyomo: model.more_tu1 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.f['tu1', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] for a in model.on) >= 10)

query: Something looks off about the overnight mix: across the whole network we're parking far more of the heavy, pricey tu2 units than of the nimble tu1s. Wouldn't a sensible plan keep the small tu1 fleet stabled overnight at least one unit ahead of the big tu2 fleet, rather than the other way round? What's the model seeing that makes leaning on the expensive units the cheaper call?
expected_pyomo: model.fleet_margin = Constraint(expr=sum(model.f['tu1', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] for a in model.on) >= sum(model.f['tu2', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] for a in model.on) + 1)

query: At several depots the plan leaves more of the big tu2 units sitting overnight than small tu1 units, which feels backwards for morning flexibility. Shouldn't every yard hold at least as many of the nimble tu1 units as it does the heavy tu2 units overnight? What's the tradeoff that's pushing the optimizer to stack the big units at those depots instead?
